title: Equilibrium Data Mining and Data Abundance
abstract: We study theoretically how the proliferation of new data (‚Äúdata abundance‚ÄĚ) affects the allocation of capital between quantitative and nonquantitative asset managers (‚Äúdata miners‚ÄĚ and ‚Äúexperts‚ÄĚ), their performance, and price informativeness. Data miners search for predictors of asset payoffs and select those with a sufficiently high precision. Data abundance raises the precision of the best predictors, but it can induce data miners to search less intensively for high-precision signals. In this case, their performance becomes more dispersed and they receive less capital. Nevertheless, data abundance always raises price informativeness and can therefore reduce asset managers' average¬†performance.

article.introduction:
‚ÄúQuants have been helped by two transformative forces. Regulatory scrutiny has made it hard to obtain an edge through methods such as prodding company executives for information [‚Ä¶] Even more importantly, investors have now at their fingertips an expanding ocean of data.‚ÄĚ(‚ÄúThe quants run Wall Street now,‚ÄĚWall Street Journal2017)
An important role of activeasset managers is to produce information about asset fundamentals. By trading on this information, they make asset prices more informative. The way asset managers generate trading signals is changing, however. An increasing number of funds (‚Äúquants‚ÄĚ) rely on quantitative techniques (statistical and computerized analysis of vast amount of data) rather than human judgment. For instance, Abis (2022) finds that quant funds account for18.6%$18.6\%$of all U.S. active equity funds in 2017 versus6.10%$6.10\%$in 2000.1Many industry commentators relate this trend to the big data revolution.2Yet, to our knowledge, the economic mechanism driving this relationship has not been formalized, leaving many questions unanswered. For instance, is the rise of quant funds inevitable? How does it affect the performance of active asset managers? And does it increase price¬†efficiency?
Our paper provides a benchmark rational expectations model to study this mechanism and address these questions. The model is unique in its ability to separate the effects of two facets of the big data revolution: (i) the proliferation of new data sets, ‚Äúdata abundance,‚ÄĚ and (ii) lower data processing costs due to improvements in computing power. These are often comingled, as if both contribute to the reduction in the cost of information production. Yet, they are conceptually distinct. Alternative data such as sensors data or social media data are useful to predict future cash flows or returns and can improve the precision of signals that quant funds discover.3However, the emergence of new data does not in and of itself reduce data processing costs. Our central finding is that the effects of data abundance and those of lower data processing costs are not necessarily the same. In particular, as we explain below, these two dimensions of the big data revolution have different implications for the dynamics of the allocation of capital to quants and discretionary¬†funds.
Our model features risk-averse investors who allocate their capital to two types of asset managers: (i) experts and (ii) data miners. These two types of asset managers produce information about a risky asset using different technologies. Each expert has a predetermined ability (skill) to obtain a signal of a given precision. In contrast, data miners discover trading signals through a search process that comprises several rounds of exploration. In each round, each data miner can combine variables from different data sets (e.g., accounting data and social media data) to build a predictor of the asset payoff. The quality of this predictor,ŌĄ$\tau$, is drawn from a distribution whose support ranges from zero (noise) toŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$(the data frontier). After each round, each data miner can decide to either launch a new round of exploration at costc$c$(the search cost) or stop searching. This process is repeated until each data miner finds a satisficing predictor. The market for the risky asset then opens and trading takes place between asset managers, dealers, and noise¬†traders.
We interpret experts as discretionary funds and data miners as quant funds. The former rely on expertise (e.g., industry knowledge) and judgmental analysis to generate investment ideas, while the latter systematically explores various data sets to discover new predictors (see Harvey et¬†al. (2017) and Abis (2022)). Quants' search process requires (i) obtaining, cleaning and preparing new data for analysis, (ii) using statistical techniques to obtain a predictor based on these data, and (iii) deciding, via backtesting, whether a predictor is good enough for live trading (see Narang (2013)). One round of exploration comprises all of these steps and the exploration cost for the round is the total cost of executing each of these¬†steps.
We characterize the equilibrium of the model, that is, (i) the allocation of capital to data miners and experts, (ii) data miners' optimal search strategy, (iii) asset managers' optimal holdings, and (iv) the risky asset price such that asset managers and investors make optimal decisions and the asset market clears. In equilibrium, each data miner optimally stops searching once she finds a predictor whose quality exceeds an endogenous threshold,ŌĄ‚ąó$\tau ^*$. Thus, in equilibrium, the quality of data miners' predictors ranges fromŌĄ‚ąó$\tau ^*$(least informative) toŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$(most informative). As a result, even though data miners are ex-ante homogeneous (same preferences and exploration costs), they are ex-post heterogeneous in the quality of their predictors and¬†performance.
When investors allocate capital to asset managers, they observe experts' skill level (signal precision) and correctly anticipate data miners' search strategy (ŌĄ‚ąó$\tau ^*$). Investors prefer to allocate capital to experts with skill aboveŌĄ‚ąó$\tau ^*$than to data miners because their expected utility increases with asset managers' skill. However, each expert's capacity to serve investors is limited. Investors therefore optimally allocate capital to experts up to the point at which the marginal expert has a signal quality just equal toŌĄ‚ąó$\tau ^*$and each expert operates at full capacity. The rest of investor capital is optimally allocated to data miners. This process determines the fraction of investor capital, denoted asőľ‚ąó$\mu ^*$, allocated to data¬†miners.
Our novel implications derive from the effect of increasing the data frontierŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$(e.g., due to the availability of new data sets) or reducing data miners' search costsc$c$(e.g., due to greater computing power) on their search strategy (ŌĄ‚ąó$\tau ^*$) and the allocation of capital between data miners and experts (őľ‚ąó$\mu ^*$).4
Consider a push back of the data frontier (i.e., an increase inŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$). This shock increases the expected trading profit of data miners who find the best predictor (the hidden gold nugget effect) since its quality becomes even larger. However, these data miners trade more aggressively on their signal (i.e., they make larger bets for a given deviation between the asset price and their forecast of its payoff) because they face less risk. As a result, the asset price is more informative (closer to the asset payoff), which reduces the expected profit of all data miners (the price informativeness effect). The first effect raises the value of searching for a better predictor after finding one while the second reduces it. WhenŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$is small, the hidden gold nugget effect dominates. Thus, a push back of the data frontier induces data miners to demand higher quality predictors (ŌĄ‚ąó$\tau ^*$increases in equilibrium) and investors shift capital from experts to data miners. However, whenŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$becomes large enough, a push back of the data frontier induces data miners to search less intensively (ŌĄ‚ąó$\tau ^*$decreases) and capital flows back to experts because the price informativeness effect¬†dominates.
In contrast, a decrease in data miners' search cost unambiguously raises the value of searching for another predictor after finding one because it reduces the total expected cost of search without directly affecting data miners' average trading aggressiveness. Thus, a reduction in data miners' search costs always leads them to be more demanding with respect to the quality of their predictors and induces investors to allocate relatively more capital to these¬†funds.
In sum, the model explains the growth in the relative size of quant funds by both a decrease in search costs (due, e.g., to improvements in computing power) and data abundance. However, it predicts that as the amount of data continues to grow, this trend should reverse itself. Thus, data abundance is both a catalyst for and a limit on the rise of quant¬†funds.
In equilibrium, a push back of the data frontier always triggers an improvement in the average quality of the signals used by all asset managers (even though it can reduce data miners' search intensity), as does a reduction in data miners' search costs. Thus, a push back of the data frontier or a decrease in data miners' search costs increases the informativeness of the risky asset price. For these reasons, asset managers' (cross-sectional) average performance (as measured by their average gross excess returns) is hump-shaped in data miners' search costs (c$c$) and the data frontier (ŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$). Indeed, the improvement in the average quality of asset managers' signals has a positive effect on asset managers' average performance, while the increase in price informativeness has a negative effect (because it reduces each asset manager's informational edge). WhenŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$becomes large enough orc$c$small enough, the second effect dominates. Thus, in the long run, the big data revolution is expected to erode active asset managers' performance (quants and discretionary funds alike).
Shocks to the data frontier and data miners' search costs should also affect the dispersion of performance within a group of asset managers (experts or data miners). The average performance of an asset manager increases with the quality of her signal, other things equal. Thus, within a group, the difference between the average performance of top and bottom performers increases in the difference between the quality of their signals. A reduction in data miners' search costs raises the lowest quality of the signals used by both data miners and experts, directly in the former case (through a more intense search process) and indirectly in the latter case (through a reallocation of capital away from the experts with the lowest skill). As a result, the difference in the performance between top and bottom performers within each group is inversely related to data miners' search cost. In contrast, this difference is a U-shaped function of the data frontier,ŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$. Indeed, whenŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$is small, a push back of the data frontier increases data miners' search intensity while it reduces it whenŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$becomes large¬†enough.
We also analyze how these shocks affect management fees, assuming that they are set through Nash bargaining (as in Garleanu and Pedersen (2018)). In equilibrium, data miners charge a fee equal to their reservation wage. Intuitively, they cannot extract rents from investors because all data miners offer the same ex-ante expected utility to each investor. In contrast, the higher is an expert's skill, the higher is the expected utility of investors allocating capital to this expert. As experts with a given skill are in short supply (due to limited capacity), those with a skill level above the marginal expert's skill,ŌĄ‚ąó$\tau ^*$, can charge a fee above their reservation wage. A decrease in data miners' search costs reduces experts' fees because it raises data miners' search intensity and therefore the expected utility that investors obtain by allocating their funds to them (investors' outside option when they negotiate fees with experts). This is also the case for an increase in the data frontier when it induces data miners to search more intensively. However, when it does not, a push back of the data frontier enables low-quality experts to charge larger fees because allocating capital to data miners becomes less¬†attractive.
To our knowledge, our predictions regarding data abundance are new (see SectionI). In contrast, the effects of variation in data miners' search cost (c$c$) are similar to those obtained by varying the cost of information acquisition in standard noisy rational expectations models (e.g., Verrecchia (1982)). However, we are not aware of models in which, in equilibrium, investors with the same information acquisition technology acquire signals of different precision, as is the case for data miners in our model. This feature of our model enables us to derive novel implications for the effects of shocks toc$c$(orŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$) on the cross-sectional dispersion in asset managers' performance (see SectionV).5
The paper is organized as follows. SectionIpositions our contribution in the literature. SectionIIpresents the model and SectionIIIderives its equilibrium. SectionIVanalyzes how changes in data miners' search costs and data abundance affect the allocation of capital to data miners and the informativeness of the asset price. SectionVderives implications for asset managers' performance. In SectionVI, we endogenize asset managers' fees, and we show that the implications of the model are robust to arbitrary fund sizes. SectionVIIfurther discusses the testable implications of our theory. SectionVIIIconcludes.
article.I. Contribution to the Literature:
Our paper is related to three strands of literature. First, it contributes to the theoretical literature studying trends in the asset management industry (such as the rise of institutional investors, institutional holdings' concentration, or indexing) and their effects (e.g., Huang, Qiu, and Yang (2019), Bond and Garc√≠a (2021), Buss and Sundaresan (2022), and Kacperczyk, Nosal, and Sundaresan (2024)). Here, we focus on the rise of quant funds and relate it to (i) the availability of new data and (ii) a decline in the cost of processing this data. We are not aware of other papers that do¬†so.
The theoretical literature on quantitative investors is scarce. Malikov and Pasquariello (2022) introduce a quantitative informed investor to the model of Kyle (1985). This investor is myopic in the sense that her trading strategy is optimal, provided that other informed investors behave as if she was not present. This assumption captures the idea that quantitative investors follow fixed trading rules calibrated on past data (‚Äúbacktesting‚ÄĚ). In contrast, we formalize quants as agents that produce information through a search process and we focus on the effects of both data abundance and information processing costs on their strategies (these parameters play no role in Malikov and Pasquariello (2022)). Our modeling approach is closer to Garleanu and Pedersen (2018). In their model, active asset managers can be either informed or uninformed and they study how investors allocate capital to both types. Investors can identify informed asset managers at a cost. Here, we assume that identifying informed asset managers is costless but we introduce heterogeneity in the technologies used by active asset managers to generate their investment¬†ideas.
Second, our paper contributes to the literature on endogenous information acquisition in noisy rational expectations models (see Veldkamp (2011) for a survey). These models assume that either (i) investors can obtain a signal of fixed exogenous precision by paying a cost (e.g., Grossman and Stiglitz (1980)) or (ii) investors choose the precision of their signal, with signals of higher precision being more costly (e.g., Verrecchia (1982)). Neither of these approaches can be used to study how an exogenous shock on the distribution of signals' precision affects agents' efforts to improve the precision of their signals (the intensive margin of information production). Under the first approach, only the extensive margin of information production (the mass of investors acquiring information) is endogenous. Under the second approach, the intensive margin varies endogenously but only through shocks to the cost of information production. Yet, as explained in the introduction, the availability of new data sets enables investors to find signals of higher quality without a change in data processing costs. Thus, to analyze the effects of data abundance on investors' efforts to discover signals of high quality, one needs a new modeling¬†approach.
Our methodological contribution is to propose such an approach. Our framework allows us to analyze the effect of exogenous shocks on investors' signals precision (shocks toŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\text{max}}_{dm}$)‚ÄĒholding the cost of information production (c$c$) constant‚ÄĒon both (i) the intensive margin of information production (data miners' search intensity) and (ii) the extensive margin (the allocation of capital between two types of informed investors). In this way, we can untangle the effects of the two dimensions of the big data revolution: (i) a reduction in the cost of processing data and (ii) an increase in the precision of the best signals that investors can obtain due to an expansion of available data. We find that these two shocks can affect efforts to produce information in opposite directions. To our knowledge, this economic insight is new and cannot be delivered by the existing literature (since it does not allow to analyze (ii) separately from (i)).
Han and Sangiorgi (2018) and Banerjee and Breon-Drish (2021) also formalize information acquisition as a search problem but they analyze different questions. Han and Sangiorgi (2018) provide a microfoundation for the assumption that information acquisition costs are increasing and convex in precision (e.g., Verrecchia (1982)). In Banerjee and Breon-Drish (2021), one investor optimally alternates between periods in which she searches for information and periods in which she does not. When she searches for information, the investor finds a signal of a given precision according to a Poisson process and starts trading on this signal as soon as she finds it. In this model, investors face uncertainty on forgone trading opportunities (due to search delays) rather than signal quality as in our¬†model.
Lastly, our paper contributes to the growing theoretical literature on the effects of new technologies for producing financial information (e.g., Dugast and Foucault (2018), Farboodi and Veldkamp (2020), Milhet (2020), and Huang, Xiong, and Yang (2022)). This literature assumes that information technology progress reduces the cost of processing information (or relaxes investors' attention constraints) and explores ramifications of this assumption. Our model accounts for another dimension of this progress, namely, improvement in the precision of the signals that investors can obtain due to new data (data abundance).

We interpret experts as asset managers relying on judgmental analysis to generate investment ideas (so-called discretionary investors) and data miners as managers relying on a quantitative approach to obtain trading signals (quant funds). A predictor can be viewed as a function (e.g., fitted with linear regressions or ML techniques) of variables from different data sets (e.g., accounting data, geolocation data, and consumer transactions data) that minimizes the predictor's average forecasting error in-sample. article.IV. Capital Allocation: Data Miners vers:
In this section, we study how a reduction in data miners' search costs (c$c$) or a push back of the data frontier (an increase inŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$) affect data miners' search intensity (ŌĄ‚ąó$\tau ^*$), the allocation of capital to data miners relative to experts (őľ‚ąó$\mu ^*$), the average quality of asset managers' signals (ŌĄ¬Į$\bar{\tau }$), and price informativeness (I$\cal {I}$). We are particularly interested in the effects that arise when the cost of search becomes very small or data become very abundant (ŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$very large), as these limiting cases are often described as the endpoint of current progress in information technology (Nordhaus (2021)).

For this reason, a decrease in data miners' search costs raises the allocation capital to data miners (őľ‚ąó$\mu ^*$). article.VII. Empirical Implications:
The last 20 years have witnessed tremendous advances in information technology, with a sharp decline in computing costs (enabling the use of machine learning techniques) and steady growth of so-called alternative data sets (such as social media data, credit card data, and satellite imagery).32In our model, this evolution corresponds to a decrease in data miners' information processing costs (c$c$) and a push back of the data frontier (ŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$). TableIsummarizes the implications of our model regarding the effects of these¬†parameters.
A first implication is that this trend (the decline inc$c$and the increase inŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$) should increase the capital allocated to quants (data miners in our model) relative to discretionary funds, provided thatŌĄdmmax‚Č§ŌĄtr(c)$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}\le \tau ^{tr}(c)$(Propositions4and5). This is consistent with the rise of quant funds since the turn of the millennium (see Harvey et¬†al. (2017), Abis (2022), and Dyer, Guest, and Yu (2022)).
Another implication is that the decline in information processing costs and data abundance should trigger an improvement in price informativeness (I$\cal {I}$). Consistent with this implication, Zhu (2019) finds that the availability of satellite imagery and consumer-browsing data‚ÄĒa positive shock toŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$‚ÄĒimproves price informativeness for the stocks covered by such data (see also Bonelli and Foucault (2023)). An additional implication, yet untested, is that this improvement should be due to an increase in the average quality of active asset managers' signals (even though the dispersion in quality could increase).33
A decrease in information processing costs (c$c$) or a push back of the data frontier (ŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$) can also reduce the (cross-sectional) average performance of all active asset managers (see Corollary1and Figure3). In line with this implication, Bonelli and Foucault (2023) find that the availability of satellite imagery data reduces active funds' average excess returns in stocks covered by these data (U.S. retailers' stocks).
These findings provide only suggestive (and fragmentary) evidence consistent with the model. Literature on the effects of big data on active asset managers is still scarce. Tests of our theory should focus on its most novel predictions, namely, (i) the availability of new data can trigger a decline in the capital allocated to quant funds and reduce the difference between the performance of top and bottom performers in a given group of funds (data miners or quants), while (ii) a drop inc$c$should have the opposite effects (see Corollary3and Figure4). For these tests, empiricists could (i) classify active funds into quants and discretionary funds, using, for instance, methodologies and data similar to those used by Harvey et¬†al. (2017), Abis (2022), and Dyer, Guest, and Yu (2022) and (ii) exploit natural experiments that increase the amount of available data (raiseŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$) or reduce information processing costs (c$c$). We give examples of such experiments¬†below.
The availability of alternative data sets varies across industries and asset classes (see Kolanovic and Smith (2019) and SectionIV.Cin Dessaint, Foucault, and Fr√©sard (2024)). For example, there are far more alternative data sets covering firms in the consumer industry than in health care or covering equity than commodities. As a result, quant funds are differentially exposed to the rise of alternative data sets. Empiricists could exploit this feature to test whether, consistent with our predictions, variation in funds' exposure to alternative data (due to heterogeneity in their holdings) explains (i) the dispersion in funds' performance within a given group (e.g., quant funds) and (ii) the allocation of capital between quant funds and discretionary funds operating in the same industry or asset class. With this approach, identification of the effects of data abundance on the variables of interest (e.g., the dispersion in funds' performance) would come from cross-sectional variations in funds' exposure to alternative data (shocks toŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$).34
For shocks toc$c$, empiricists could use technological changes that reduce the cost of data processing, such as the introduction of cloud computing by Amazon with its release of Amazon Web Services (AWS) in 2006 (see Ewens, Nanda, and Rhodes-Kropf (2018)) or the Securities and Exchange Commission's 2009 eXtensible Business Reporting Language (XBLR) mandate. The XBLR mandate requires firms to provide their regulatory filings in machine-readable form (through the EDGAR website). It did not change the information content of regulatory filings (firms had to provide the same data before and after the mandate) and thus did not changeŌĄdmmax$\tau ^{\rm max}_{dm}$. However, it reduced the cost of automated data processing (e.g., using Natural Language Processing techniques; see Bhattacharya, Cho, and Kim (2018) and Zhao (2021)) and thereforec$c$(quantitative funds rely on information in regulatory filings; see Barbopoulos et¬†al. (2021) for evidence). As the introduction of the XBLR mandate was staggered, funds were differentially exposed to it, depending on their holdings. Thus, the XBLR mandate offers another way to study the effect of a reduction in information processing costs for quant funds, holding available data constant.35
article.VIII. Conclusion:
We study theoretically the effects of the big data revolution on the production of information by active asset managers, their performance, and asset price informativeness. Our model features two types of asset managers, namely, experts (which we interpret as discretionary funds) and data miners (which we interpret as quant funds). Experts have fixed ability to generate trading signals of a given precision about a risky asset, while data miners obtain trading signals through a search process with the goal of discovering the best (i.e., highest-precision) trading signals. We assume that the big data revolution reduces data miners' search costs and enables them to increase the precision of the best signals (the data frontier).
We study in detail how these changes affect, in equilibrium, (i) data miners' optimal search strategy, (ii) the allocation of capital between data miners and experts, (iii) asset price informativeness, (iv) asset managers' average performance, (v) the cross-sectional dispersion in asset managers' performance, and (vi) asset managers' fees. This analysis offers a framework to interpret how the rise of quant funds relates to recent advances in information technology and its consequences for price informativeness and active asset managers'¬†performance.
Importantly, the model allows us to separately analyze the effects of reducing data miners' costs (e.g., due to greater computing power) and the effects of the data frontier being pushed back (due to new data availability). We show that these two distinct dimensions of the big data revolution do not necessarily have the same implications. For instance, the capital allocated to data miners always rises with a decline in their search costs, while it is a hump-shaped function of the data frontier. This highlights the importance of distinguishing shocks that affect information processing costs from shocks that affect the amount of available data in empirical analyses of the effects of the big data revolution on financial¬†markets.
